channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: This Startup Fused Human Brain Cells with Silicon Chips | E2295
gist: Two deep-tech founder interviews: Cortical Labs (Hon Weng Chong) on commercializing 'biological computing' — silicon chips fused with lab-grown human neurons, now in a 120-unit 'biological data center' and raising $30M — and Pyka (Michael Norcia) on large autonomous electric/hybrid cargo and crop-spray drones deployed at scale in Brazil, with a defense-oriented hybrid-diesel 'Dropship' variant. Both surface the same investor thesis: hardware-software-fused systems gated by real-world deployment and regulation, not by code.

themes.theme: Cortical Labs commercial traction & business model

insights.claim: Cortical Labs sold out its entire capped run of 30 CL1 biological-computing units at ~$35,000 each (roughly $1M revenue), with units now deployed at five US institutions including Johns Hopkins, Mass General, UCSF, and Dartmouth.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO, 'Han')
insights.so_what: Validated early demand and brand-name research customers de-risk a category most investors would dismiss as science fiction; the $35K hardware price anchors unit economics for the category.
insights.quote: We've kind of exhausted our entire stock of 30 units that we had capped.

insights.claim: Cortical Labs is raising a $30M round, framed around scaling its 'Cortical Cloud' rather than hardware sales, to lower the accessibility barrier to biological computing the way free CUDA did for GPUs.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: A live raise plus an explicit 'be the CUDA of wetware' platform thesis is the core investable narrative; watch whether cloud access compounds a developer ecosystem.
insights.quote: we're raising 30

insights.claim: 90% of Cortical Labs' purchases come from academic R&D institutions, with stated primary use cases in drug discovery, disease modeling (Alzheimer's/dementia at Mass General), toxicology and animal-testing alternatives (Hopkins), and movement disorders (UCSF).
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Near-term TAM is biomedical research / animal-testing replacement, not AI compute; the 'net good' framing also serves as regulatory/ethical cover for the more speculative compute use case.

insights.claim: Cortical Labs has deployed 120 CL1 units in its Melbourne 'biological data center' but only ~20 cloud users are live (with ~10 more pending), citing 'teething' issues growing neurons and a ~2-month lead time from cell-culture decision to usable compute.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Biological supply-chain latency is the real bottleneck on revenue ramp; capacity utilization (~20/120) is the key operational metric to track before the next raise.
insights.quote: while we do have 120, only about 20 or 30 users are on the cloud at the moment. Actually, no, 20 users.

insights.claim: Each CL1 currently serves one customer/workload at a time (no chaining yet), but Cortical Labs is developing PDMS microfluidic chiplet partitioning to virtualize a single chip into many isolated neuron wells — a VMware-style multi-tenancy play to raise yield per unit.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Multi-tenant virtualization would step-change cloud gross margins and is the path from boutique hardware to scalable SaaS-like economics; it is still R&D, not shipping.
insights.quote: maybe you can get a hyper like a virtual machine with multiple people running the same thing. So that way we can increase yield

themes.theme: Biocomputing tech edge: sample efficiency & energy

insights.claim: In unpublished reinforcement-learning path-finding benchmarks with a research partner, Cortical Labs' neurons were ~5,000x more sample-efficient than the partner's GPU-based RL systems, meaning a biological unit learns in one step what a GPU needs ~5,000 steps to learn.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Sample efficiency is the exact pain point in modern RL/robotics; a verified 5,000x edge (pending NeurIPS-style publication later this year) would be a genuine non-obvious threat to GPU-bound RL workloads.
insights.quote: the neurons we had were 5,000 times more sample efficient than their GPU based systems.

insights.claim: GPUs can offset their RL sample-inefficiency by accelerating simulated time ~5,000x, but that advantage collapses for embodied/robotic agents that must operate at real-world speed — pointing biological compute at robotics RL specifically.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Identifies the wedge where biocompute could beat silicon: real-time embodied learning where GPUs can't fast-forward the clock; a watch-item for robotics-RL investors.
insights.quote: you can't accelerate time if you're a robot. You you're operating at the same speed like everyone else in the physical world.

insights.claim: A single CL1 unit draws only ~30 watts, letting data-center partners add biological compute without touching their power and cooling budgets — a structural advantage as AI data centers hit energy caps.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: In a power-constrained AI buildout, ~30W compute that bolts onto existing racks under fixed megawatt caps is a real commercial hook for data-center operators.
insights.quote: one unit only uses about 30 watts of energy

insights.claim: Chong frames biology — even a fly or cockroach — as possessing 'generalized intelligence' (e.g. real-time evasion/prediction) that today's 'super-intelligent but not generally intelligent' LLMs like GPT-5.5 lack, positioning biocompute as a path to AGI-adjacent generalization without human-level neuron counts.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: A contrarian framing that generalization, not raw scale, is the missing ingredient — if it holds, it reframes where AGI capex should flow away from pure transformer scaling.
insights.quote: even very simple organisms like you know a fly has generalized intelligence um something that none of our machines have

insights.claim: Chong argues the current limit on biological computing is algorithmic (how to encode digital/internet information into analog neural systems), not hardware — the same write-to-neuron problem that brain-computer-interface firms Neuralink and Synchron are solving.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Casts Cortical Labs as adjacent to/benefiting from BCI R&D (Neuralink, Synchron); algorithmic breakthroughs there could be read-throughs for biocompute, and vice versa.

insights.claim: Cortical Labs' next biological data center is in Singapore via data-center partner Day One, sited under a 200MW government energy cap, scaling to ~1,000 CL1 units (~200,000 neurons each), with an on-site lab to grow neurons locally.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: A named data-center partner and a 10x capacity jump (120 to ~1,000 units) is the concrete scaling milestone; Day One's motivation (compute outside the power budget) is a reusable go-to-market for the category.
insights.quote: they've built not just the space to house a thousand CR1 units, but next to it a laboratory for us to grow the cells for the compute on site.

insights.claim: Growing neurons on-site decouples each biological data center from a single overseas chip vendor, making each facility self-sufficient and removing supply-chain constraints — a 'sovereign compute' angle distinct from GPU-vendor concentration.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Pitches biocompute as a vendor-independent, geopolitically resilient alternative to Nvidia-dependent AI infrastructure — a differentiated narrative for sovereign-AI and supply-chain-anxious buyers.
insights.quote: every data center technically is not reliant on one vendor from one country.

insights.claim: CL1 neurons can live a long time if maintained; the recurring consumable is the filtration ('kidney') tube-set cartridges, swapped every 4–6 months — defining the razor-and-blade aftermarket of the platform.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Consumable cartridge replacement is the recurring-revenue / maintenance-cost line item that determines true cloud margins and customer lock-in.

insights.claim: Cortical Labs draws a hard 'red line' against creating conscious systems because a conscious system could suffer, using stem-cell-derived neurons (not harvested brains) and engaging bioethicists and even the Vatican proactively to pre-empt ethical backlash.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Proactive ethics engagement is a deliberate regulatory/PR moat for a category one scandal could kill; the consciousness 'red line' is both genuine constraint and de-risking narrative for institutional buyers and regulators.
insights.quote: You do not want to create conscious systems because um ethically uh a conscious system has the ability to suffer

insights.claim: Chong says the industry's first priority is agreeing on shared nomenclature across the biocomputing and consciousness research fields, because without common definitions there's 'a lot of fear with not much understanding' that stalls progress and adoption.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Standards-setting is an early-mover advantage; whoever frames the nomenclature shapes regulation and category perception — a soft moat for the incumbent.

insights.claim: A non-biologist student developer built a working 'Doom on neurons' demo at a Stanford hackathon using only Cortical Labs' API/SDK (pip-installable, Python/Jupyter), and was subsequently accepted into a top incubator — evidence the platform is low-barrier enough for outside developers.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: External-developer adoption with no biology background is the leading indicator of a real platform; the cloud + SDK is the flywheel the $30M raise is meant to fund.
insights.quote: the uh Doom was actually a student developer um who um uh participated in a hackathon at Stanford. no biology background, used our APN SDK

insights.claim: Chong argues today's AI boom was a 'fluke' — it took ~7 years from CUDA's creation to AlexNet — and Nvidia's real masterstroke was making CUDA free on even the cheapest gaming GPUs, an accessibility playbook Cortical Labs is explicitly copying with its cloud.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Reframes Nvidia's moat as distribution/accessibility rather than serendipity, and signals Cortical Labs' deliberate strategy to seed an ecosystem before the 'killer algorithm' arrives.
insights.quote: what Jensen did you know I think brilliantly at the start was he made CUDA free.

insights.claim: Chong is explicitly 'team human,' arguing LLMs will not solve biocomputing applications and human developers remain essential to invent use cases — flagging upcoming hackathons at MIT to recruit talent.
insights.speaker: Hon Weng Chong (Cortical Labs CEO)
insights.so_what: Talent/developer recruiting is the gating input for ecosystem growth; hackathon pipeline at MIT is a concrete signal of where the next applications (and hires) come from.
insights.quote: I'm on team human here, so we still need developers to think of really cool applications. The LMS are not going to solve this for us.

themes.theme: Pyka: large autonomous drones & deployment moat

insights.claim: Pyka claims to be the only company on Earth deploying Group 4 UAS (drones over 1,320 lbs) to commercial customers at scale, operating exclusively in rural Brazil where customers run 7-day-a-week, 12–13-hour shifts.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: A defensible 'only at scale' position in heavy autonomous cargo/ag drones; real-world operating hours are the proprietary data moat competitors lack.
insights.quote: we're actually the only company on Earth that I'm aware of that have deployed a group for UAS to commercial customers at scale.

insights.claim: Pyka's Pelican ag drone starts at ~$550,000 with a roughly 2–3 year payback, but its core value is spray quality and a cheaper labor pool — operators trainable in 2–4 weeks versus ~18 months for a manned aerial-application pilot.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: Concrete unit economics and a labor-arbitrage moat (weeks vs. 18 months of pilot training) define the adoption case; payback period is the metric for ag-tech ROI underwriting.
insights.quote: you can train someone to operate a Pelican in 2 to 4 weeks versus a aerial application pilot is you know 18 months

insights.claim: The chemical sprayed costs ~4x the cost of application, so Pyka's edge is even, precise deposition that reduces waste of an expensive input — amplified now that a blocked Strait of Hormuz is spiking global fertilizer prices.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: Ties drone ROI to volatile agrochemical input costs; rising fertilizer/chemical prices (Hormuz disruption) strengthen the precision-spray value prop and demand timing.
insights.quote: the cost of the chemical that is being sprayed is typically about four times the cost of the application.

insights.claim: Pyka's electric Pelican burns ~2 gallons/hour even when charged off a dirty diesel generator, versus the competing manned Air Tractor's ~55 gallons of jet fuel per hour — a ~25x fuel-efficiency advantage.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: Order-of-magnitude operating-cost advantage versus the incumbent crop-duster aircraft is the hard quantitative case for switching, fuel-price-resilient.
insights.quote: our uh the Pelican in the worst case if you're charging it off of a diesel generator is about 2 gall per hour.

insights.claim: US adoption requires ~20,000-acre farms to fully utilize a Pelican (very large by US standards, midsize by Brazil's), pointing to a 'spray-as-a-service' contractor model in the US where one operator services many smaller farms in a radius.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals the US business model will be service/utility, not unit sales — relevant for sizing US TAM and the eventual recurring-revenue structure.

themes.theme: Regulation as the binding constraint on autonomous flight

insights.claim: Pyka holds the largest drone approved for US commercial use by the FAA, but is capped at ~4km from takeoff (vs. 5–15km in Brazil), which Norcia calls 'not commercially viable today'; beyond-visual-line-of-sight rules are the gating constraint and Pyka is working with the FAA to expand the limit.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: US regulatory timeline — not technology — is the swing factor for Pyka's domestic revenue; FAA BVLOS rulemaking is the catalyst to watch for the whole large-drone sector.
insights.quote: We can only fly the drone about 4 kilometers away from where it took off and landed from, which is not commercially viable today.

insights.claim: Brazil deregulated agricultural drones ~2 years ago, which is the primary reason Pyka deploys there; Norcia argues the US is 'shooting itself in the foot' by failing to enable scaled BVLOS operations on a relevant timeline.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: Regulatory arbitrage (Brazil, Rwanda for Zipline, Ukraine for combat drones) is where real-world deployment data is being captured — a recurring pattern investors can use to find deployable hardware companies.
insights.quote: Brazil actually deregulated agricultural drones about 2 years ago, which is very convenient for us.

insights.claim: Norcia generalizes that hardware-software-blended products' value is '100% determined by their exposure to the real world,' citing SpaceX (blew up rockets to learn), Zipline (Rwanda), and Ukraine combat-drone makers as the few firms that found a path to collect deployment data.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: A reusable diligence lens: deep-tech hardware is only as valuable as its real-world flight/operating data — screen for companies that have unlocked a deployment geography.
insights.quote: their value is 100% determined by their exposure to the real world.

insights.claim: Pyka built its hybrid turbo-diesel 'Dropship' from CAD to first flight in 180 days as a dual-use logistics aircraft, hitting Air Force/SOCOM/Army-derived requirements: ~1,000-mile range with 500-lb payload, fits a 20-ft container, removable tail, heavy-fuel (JP5/diesel) operation, and air-droppable payloads.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: A defense-spec product hitting unwritten contested-logistics requirements opens a far larger DoD TAM than ag; 180-day CAD-to-flight is the speed signal that attracts defense-tech capital.
insights.quote: we went from uh like initial CAD renderings to first flight in 180 days.

insights.claim: Pyka sent three electric Pelican Cargo aircraft to the Air Force and one to the Army, received positive feedback, a follow-on contract interest from the Air Force, and invitations to major demos in ~2.5 months — concrete early defense traction.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: Tangible government pilot deployments and follow-on contract signals are leading indicators of a defense revenue ramp; the upcoming demos are a near-term catalyst.

insights.claim: Pyka deliberately did not optimize Dropship as a low-cost kinetic bomber — its vehicles are 'too nice,' reliable and long-lived — choosing the reusable contested-logistics niche over expendable munitions, a different defense positioning than attritable attack drones.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: Clarifies Pyka's defense segment as reusable logistics, not the crowded expendable-FPV-strike market — distinct competitive set and procurement logic.
insights.quote: our vehicles last too long. Basically, they're too nice.

themes.theme: Vertical integration, supply chain & VC dynamics

insights.claim: Pyka vertically integrated nearly every critical component (motors, batteries, motor controllers, avionics, airframes) because off-the-shelf parts didn't exist when it started, letting it now ship NDAA-compliant versions and manufacture battery management systems in the US for defense.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: China-free, NDAA-compliant vertical integration is a defense procurement moat and de-risks supply chains — a key diligence checkbox for defense-hardware investors.
insights.quote: we actually vertically integrated essentially every critical component on our product.

insights.claim: Norcia quantifies the cost of supply-chain sovereignty: if you own the design, US vs. China manufacturing is ~2x; if you don't own the design and buy from an OEM, US vs. China is ~4x — so design ownership roughly halves the reshoring cost penalty.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: A concrete reshoring cost framework: owning IP cuts the US-manufacturing premium from ~4x to ~2x — useful for evaluating any hardware firm's China-decoupling economics.
insights.quote: If you don't own the design and you're buying from an OEM... it's probably gonna be like 4x difference.

insights.claim: Norcia says current VC capital is flowing heavily to 'enterprise agent orchestration / MCP server' and AI-wrapper SaaS startups (which he urges VCs to stop backing), implying capital is mispriced away from deployable deep-tech hardware like drones.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: A founder-side read on capital rotation: crowded AI-agent/SaaS funding vs. relatively under-capitalized deep-tech hardware — a potential contrarian allocation signal.
insights.quote: Stop backing SAS companies that are slapping on an AI rapper. Back something cool. We're building drones, y'all.

insights.claim: Norcia argues the most successful hardware companies (Apple, Tesla, DJI) win by owning both hardware and software to deliver a 'magical' simple experience, and warns that integrating disparate third-party systems produces a visibly worse product — implicitly framing Boeing's outsource-and-integrate model as the cautionary failure.
insights.speaker: Michael Norcia (Pyka founder/CEO)
insights.so_what: A reusable thesis that full-stack hardware-software ownership is the durable moat in hardware (Apple/Tesla/DJI) and outsourced integration is the path to Boeing-style decline — a screen for hardware investments.
insights.quote: you have to own the hardware and the software in my opinion. like that's how you make that really magical experience.
